abstract.text: Program a STM32 microcontroller directly with low-level Swift code

metadata.title: Baremetal Setup for STM32 with Embedded Swift

inlineContent.text: This tutorial will guide you through setting up a baremetal STM32 project with Embedded Swift to create a simple LED blinking application with text output to UART, the “Hello World” of embedded systems. Concretely, we’ll be using the STM32F746G-DISCO (discovery) development board, but the setup will work (with only small tweaks) on most other STM32 devices.

We will be writing a full firmware from scratch including low-level boot code, direct hardware register configuration, a custom linker script and more. Besides educational purposes, this level of control is typically only needed in specialized setups because it involves more work and more complexity. If you want a simpler path to get started, you can integrate with an existing embedded SDK (like STCube or BSPs) instead, which offers higher-level APIs and hardware abstraction. Check out
for guidance on common integration patterns.
The entire baremetal project will consist of:
A Package.swift file describing the overall structure of the project
Register definitions for GPIO registers generated by Swift MMIO
A toolset.json file defining compilation and linking flags
A Makefile that will serve as a simple shortcut for building and flashing
A simple linker script
An interrupt vector and a reset function implementing basic startup code
Finally, the application logic in Embedded Swift that will set up UART and print “Hello World”
Let’s get started!
Mac or Linux
STM32F746G-DISCO board connected over a USB cable
Swift toolchain installed using swiftly

Edit the

content.text: Step 4: Generate MMIO register descriptions using SVD2Swift

inlineContent.text: SVD2Swift is a tool provided by Swift MMIO that automatically generates Swift code from SVD (System View Description) files. SVD files contain detailed descriptions of all the memory-mapped registers in a microcontroller, making them invaluable for embedded development. Using this generated code gives us type-safe access to the hardware registers of the STM32F7 microcontroller.

First, we’ll build the SVD2Swift tool from the swift-mmio package we added as a dependency, then download an SVD file for our specific microcontroller, and finally generate the register definitions we need:

code: --peripherals RCC USART1 GPIOA GPIOB GPIOC GPIOD GPIOE GPIOF GPIOG GPIOH GPIOI GPIOJ GPIOK

inlineContent.text: Tip: If a build fails for any reason, it’s often useful to add the
flag to
to see a full list of commands the build system runs.
Create a
file in the project directory to configure the build with essential settings for ARMv7-based STM32 microcontrollers. This file defines options for the Swift compiler, C compiler, and linker:
After this is done, let’s attempt a build using
. We have to specify the path to the toolset file, and also the right target triple:
Currently, this should succeed during compilation, but fail to link (because we haven’t yet defined a valid linker script for embedded usage):

code: error: link command failed with exit code 1 (use -v to see invocation)
ld.lld: error: unable to find library -lc
ld.lld: error: unable to find library -lm

content.text: Step 6: Create a Linker Script and Startup Code

inlineContent.text: Let’s now create a linker script, an interrupt vector and startup code.
Create a linker script file named
in the project root directory:

code: __flash_data_start = (. + 3) & ~ 3; /* 4-byte aligned end of text is where data is going to be placed (by elf2hex) */

inlineContent.text: Create startup code in C that defines the interrupt table and reset handler, in

code: *(volatile uint32_t *)0xE000ED88 |= (0xF << 20); // set CP10 and CP11 Full Access
asm volatile("bl    memcpy"); // Relocate data section to RAM
// These are provided by the linker script
// All other interrupts are not handled
// ELF entrypoint, not actually called at runtime, but it's a GC root

inlineContent.text: Finally, let’s update
to configure the linker settings for our embedded target. We’ll need to add several important options:
The
flag to specify our custom linker script
Library paths to the ARM toolchain we downloaded
Standard C library and runtime library linkage
Disable standard library and stack protector features that aren’t appropriate for baremetal code.
At this point, linking will
almost
succeed, the only unresolved reference should be stdout/putchar:
We’re not going to actually try to provide the
symbol, instead let’s provide our own custom
that will be routed to the UART.
Create a new file at

code: // A9 is UART1 TX, which is relayed by ST-LINK over USB
rcc.ahb1enr.modify { $0.raw.gpioaen = 1 }  // Enable AHB clock to port A
rcc.apb2enr.modify { $0.raw.usart1en = 1 }  // Enable APB clock to usart 1
gpioa.moder.modify { $0.raw.moder9 = 0b10 }  // Put Pin A9 into alternate function mode
gpioa.otyper.modify { $0.raw.ot9 = 0b0 }  // Put Pin A9 into push pull
gpioa.ospeedr.modify { $0.raw.ospeedr9 = 0b00 }  // Put Pin A9 into low speed
gpioa.pupdr.modify { $0.raw.pupdr9 = 0b00 }  // Disable pull up/down on Pin A9
gpioa.afrh.modify { $0.raw.afrh9 = 0b0111 }  // Set alternate function usart1 on Pin A9
// Configure UART1, set the baud rate to 115200 (we boot at 16 MHz)
// Spin while tx buffer not empty
public func putchar(_ value: CInt) -> CInt {

content.text: Step 8: Package up and Boot the Firmware

inlineContent.text: The firmware should now build successfully, and we are finally ready to boot the firmware! We’re going to use the
script to convert the ELF file that
produced into a format that’s suitable for flashing, and then we’ll use the
tool from the opensource stlink package to actually run the firmware. We’ll also use the
program to receive the text over UART, which will be presented on the host system as a serial port (also known as “COM port”).
First let’s make sure we have st-link and minicom installed:

code: This program is free software; you can redistribute it and/or
modify it under the terms of the GNU General Public License
as published by the Free Software Foundation; either version
2 of the License, or (at your option) any later version.

inlineContent.text: Then let’s fetch the elf2hex tool:
Next, let’s build, package, and flash the firmware:

Flashing should succeed and the firmware will run, but at this point, you won’t see any UART output yet, and that’s expected. While we’ve created the functions to initialize UART and transmit data, we haven’t actually called the initialization routine from our main application. The LED won’t blink either since we haven’t configured the GPIO pins for it. In the next step, we’ll update our main application file to call our UART initialization function and configure the LED pin properly.

content.text: Step 9: Configuring pins for UART and LED

inlineContent.text: Now let’s update the main application file to actually use our UART setup and blink an LED. Open the
file and replace its contents with:

code: // Enable clock for GPIO port I
print("Hello from Embedded Swift on STM32F7!")
// Main loop - toggle LED and print message
// This is not accurate timing, just a busy-wait

inlineContent.text: Now we need to create a Makefile to simplify the build and flash process. Create a file named
in the project root:

code: # Serial port for UART output viewing (change to match your system)

inlineContent.text: Now you can build and flash your firmware with a single command:

To view the UART output, determine which serial port your STM32F7 board appears as on your system. The ST-Link on the discovery board presents itself as a USB-to-Serial device. Once you’ve identified the correct port (update the SERIAL_PORT variable in the Makefile if needed), run:
You should now see “Hello from Embedded Swift on STM32F7!” followed by LED status messages in the minicom terminal, and the LED on your board should be blinking.

Congratulations! You’ve successfully set up and programmed an STM32 microcontroller in baremetal mode using Embedded Swift. This simple LED blinking project demonstrates the fundamental concepts of embedded programming:
Setting up hardware registers
Configuring GPIO pins
Creating delay functions
Implementing a main loop
From here, you can expand the project to include more complex functionality like interfacing with sensors, implementing communication protocols, or adding user input.
Add button input to control the LED patterns
Implement proper timer-based delays instead of the busy-wait approach
Add UART communication to send debug messages to your computer
Explore other peripherals like ADC, I2C, or SPI
Happy embedded programming with Swift!

abstract.text: Embedded Swift is a compilation and language mode that enables development of baremetal, embedded and standalone software in Swift
Understand the common patterns and approaches for integrating Swift with existing embedded systems
Tutorial for targetting a Raspberry Pi Pico as an embedded device that runs a simple Swift program
Possible directions to explore to start using Embedded Swift
Tutorial for building a simple program for your host OS with Embedded Swift
